Advanced AI features including semantic search

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dovetail is an AI-powered customer intelligence platform for transcribing, tagging, and analyzing user research and feedback in one searchable repository.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based EHR and practice management platform with an iPad-native app, integrated billing, and an open API for independent medical practices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DrChrono is a cloud-based electronic health record (EHR) and practice management platform built for independent medical practices, urgent care clinics, and small-to-midsize specialty groups across the United States. Founded in 2009 by Daniel Kivatinos and Michael Nusimow, the company built its early reputation on an iPad-native charting app at a time when most EHR vendors were still desktop-only, and that mobile-first design remains a core part of its identity today. It is best understood as a combined clinical-and-billing system rather than a pure documentation tool.

The platform combines clinical documentation, scheduling, e-prescribing, and medical billing and revenue cycle management (RCM) in a single system, along with an open API and app marketplace that lets practices connect DrChrono to labs, imaging providers, and other third-party healthcare tools. In November 2021, DrChrono was acquired by EverCommerce and now operates within the company's EverHealth group of healthcare software brands, though it continues to be marketed, sold, and supported as its own distinct product with its own pricing and account teams.

DrChrono is aimed squarely at U.S. ambulatory practices that need to manage clinical charting and insurance-based billing together in one system, rather than at hospitals, large enterprise health systems, or practices operating outside the U.S. healthcare and insurance framework, where its billing and coding tools would not directly apply. Practices evaluating it should think of it as one option among several EHR-plus-billing platforms aimed at independent providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who DrChrono Is For
paragraphs: DrChrono tends to fit best for solo physicians and small group practices across specialties such as primary care, urgent care, pediatrics, mental and behavioral health, and physical therapy, where having a single vendor handle both clinical charting and medical billing reduces administrative overhead and the number of systems staff need to learn. Growing multi-provider clinics that still operate as a single independent business, rather than as part of a larger health system, are also commonly cited as a good fit.

Physicians who see patients in multiple settings, such as clinic visits, hospital rounds, or house calls, are a natural fit given the platform's mobile app, which lets a provider document a visit, e-prescribe, and check a schedule directly from an iPad or iPhone without needing to return to a desktop workstation between patients. This mobility can matter as much as any single feature for providers whose day does not happen entirely at one desk.

Practices that would rather outsource billing entirely than hire and manage in-house billing staff are also a good match, since DrChrono offers plan tiers that bundle a dedicated revenue cycle management team on top of the core EHR and practice management software, rather than requiring a separate billing vendor and a separate integration project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Larger hospital systems and multi-facility health networks that need enterprise-grade interoperability, inpatient workflows, or deep integration with an existing hospital IT stack are generally better served by larger enterprise EHR vendors that are purpose-built for that scale and for hospital-based, rather than ambulatory, care. DrChrono is not positioned or marketed as an inpatient or hospital EHR.

Practices located outside the United States may find DrChrono a poor fit, since its billing, coding, and insurance-eligibility verification tools are built around U.S. payer rules and regulatory requirements rather than international healthcare and insurance systems. Practices with highly specialized documentation needs that fall outside DrChrono's template library may also need to weigh how much custom template-building work is required before going live.

Because DrChrono does not publish fixed pricing and requires a sales conversation to get a quote, practices that want fully transparent, self-serve pricing before they engage with a vendor, or that need a genuinely free or very low-cost EHR to start with almost no budget, may be better served by a vendor that advertises flat, published rates up front rather than a custom quote process.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At the clinical level, DrChrono offers customizable charting templates by specialty, dictation and speech-to-text support, and e-prescribing that includes electronic prescribing of controlled substances (EPCS), letting a clinician document a visit and send a prescription from within the same patient encounter without switching applications.

On the operational side, the platform includes appointment scheduling, patient check-in, insurance eligibility verification, and medical billing tools, ranging from claim scrubbing and electronic claim submission through to optional full-service revenue cycle management for practices that would rather have billing handled for them by DrChrono's own team.

Patients interact with the practice through the OnPatient portal for online scheduling, secure messaging, digital intake forms, and bill payment, while DrChrono's video visit tools support telehealth appointments; an open API and app marketplace round out the platform for practices that need to connect labs, imaging systems, or other specialty software to their EHR without waiting on a native integration.

long_form_blocks.heading: The DrChrono Experience
paragraphs: Because DrChrono's charting app was built for iPad from its early days, day-to-day use feels closer to a native tablet app than a typical browser-based form, which many clinicians find faster for point-of-care documentation, particularly when relying on templated notes and quick-tap exam findings during a visit rather than free-text typing.

Onboarding involves configuring templates, fee schedules, and payer connections before go-live, and practices that add the billing and RCM tiers work directly with DrChrono's billing team on claims and denial management rather than handling that work entirely in-house; user reviews describe a real learning curve for administrators setting up templates and reports, even though day-to-day charting is generally described as quick once a practice is fully configured and staff are trained.

Ongoing support and account management vary by plan tier, and since pricing and contract terms are only available by speaking with sales, practices evaluating DrChrono should expect a demo-and-quote process rather than a self-serve signup, with the overall day-to-day experience shaped heavily by which combination of EHR, billing, and outsourced RCM services a practice ultimately chooses to purchase.

features.title: iPad and iPhone native EHR app
features.description: Charting, scheduling, and e-prescribing are available through a native iOS app in addition to the web browser, letting clinicians document visits at the point of care.

features.title: Customizable clinical templates
features.description: Specialty-specific charting templates can be configured per provider, with dictation and speech-to-text support for faster note-taking.

features.title: E-prescribing with EPCS
features.description: Built-in e-prescribing supports electronic prescribing of controlled substances, so clinicians can send both standard and controlled-substance prescriptions from within the chart.

features.title: Practice management and scheduling
features.description: Appointment scheduling, patient check-in, and calendar management are integrated with the clinical record rather than run as a separate system.

features.title: Medical billing and revenue cycle management
features.description: Claim scrubbing, electronic claim submission, and denial follow-up are built in, with optional full-service RCM handled by DrChrono's own billing team for practices that want billing outsourced.

features.title: Insurance eligibility verification
features.description: Patient insurance eligibility can be checked from within the scheduling and billing workflow to reduce claim rejections before a visit.

features.title: OnPatient patient portal
features.description: A branded patient portal supports online scheduling, secure messaging, digital intake forms, and online bill payment.

features.title: Telehealth video visits
features.description: Integrated video visit tools let providers conduct telehealth appointments and document them within the same chart as in-person visits.

features.title: Open API and app marketplace
features.description: DrChrono publishes an open API and maintains a marketplace of third-party integrations, allowing practices to connect labs, imaging systems, and other specialty tools.

features.title: Reporting and analytics
features.description: Practice and billing dashboards give administrators visibility into scheduling, claims, and collections activity across providers.

pros: iPad-native charting app that feels closer to a tablet-first product than most browser-based EHR competitors
Combines clinical charting, practice management, and medical billing in one platform, reducing the number of vendors a practice must manage
Tiered plans let practices choose EHR-only, EHR-plus-billing, or a fully outsourced revenue cycle management option
E-prescribing includes controlled substances (EPCS), not just standard medications
Open API and app marketplace support integrations with labs, imaging, and other specialty software
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation, making upfront cost comparison harder
Add-on usage fees (such as texting, faxing, or automated calls) and RCM fees based on collections can make total cost less predictable
Some user reviews describe slower or inconsistent customer support response times
Setting up specialty templates, fee schedules, and payer connections can involve a real learning curve during onboarding
Built around U.S. billing, coding, and insurance workflows, so it is not well suited to practices operating outside the United States

faqs.question: Is DrChrono free to use?
faqs.answer: DrChrono is not a free product overall. It offers a no-cost base plan with limited scheduling and charting functionality, but the full EHR, e-prescribing, and billing features require a paid, custom-quoted plan.

faqs.question: How much does DrChrono cost?
faqs.answer: DrChrono does not publish fixed pricing on its website. Costs are quoted per provider per month based on which tier (EHR only, EHR plus billing, or full-service revenue cycle management) a practice selects, so practices need to contact DrChrono sales for an exact quote.

faqs.question: Does DrChrono include medical billing?
faqs.answer: Yes. DrChrono offers built-in medical billing tools such as claim scrubbing and submission on its mid-tier plans, and a full-service revenue cycle management option where DrChrono's own billing team manages claims and collections.

faqs.question: Is DrChrono HIPAA compliant?
faqs.answer: DrChrono is designed and marketed as a HIPAA-compliant EHR platform for U.S. medical practices, with the security and privacy controls expected of a system that stores protected health information.

faqs.question: Does DrChrono work outside the United States?
faqs.answer: DrChrono is built primarily around U.S. medical billing, coding, and insurance eligibility workflows, so it is generally not a strong fit for practices operating under non-U.S. healthcare and insurance systems.

use_cases.title: Solo and small independent practices
use_cases.description: A single physician or small group can run scheduling, clinical charting, e-prescribing, and billing from one connected system instead of stitching together separate tools.
use_cases.audience: Solo physicians and small independent practices

use_cases.title: Mobile and multi-site providers
use_cases.description: Clinicians who see patients across a clinic, hospital rounds, or house calls can chart, e-prescribe, and check schedules from an iPad or iPhone rather than being tied to a desktop.
use_cases.audience: Physicians who work across multiple care settings

use_cases.title: Practices outsourcing revenue cycle management
use_cases.description: Practices that would rather not staff an in-house billing team can move to a plan tier where DrChrono's own billing team manages claim submission, follow-up, and collections.

use_cases.title: Specialty groups needing tailored charting
use_cases.description: Groups in specialties like urgent care, pediatrics, or behavioral health can configure specialty-specific templates so charting matches how their clinicians actually document visits.

features: Integrated medical billing and claim submission
Full-service revenue cycle management handled by DrChrono's billing team

enrichment.seo_meta_description: DrChrono is a cloud-based EHR, practice management, and medical billing platform with an iPad-native app for independent U.S. medical practices.

enrichment.short_description: E-commerce email and SMS marketing platform with visual automation workflows and behavior-based customer segmentation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Drip is a marketing automation platform built specifically for e-commerce brands, combining email and SMS messaging with behavioral segmentation and a visual workflow builder. It started in 2013 as a simple email capture and autoresponder tool, was acquired by Leadpages in 2016, and later became an independently run company focused entirely on online retail rather than general-purpose email marketing.

The platform connects directly to store data from Shopify, WooCommerce, and similar platforms, pulling in order history, product catalogs, and browsing behavior in near real time. That data feeds into segments and automated workflows, so a customer who abandons a cart, views a product repeatedly, or crosses a lifetime-value threshold can be routed into a specific, targeted sequence of messages.

Unlike many email tools that treat SMS as an add-on, Drip built texting as a native channel that lives inside the same automation canvas as email. A single workflow can branch across both channels based on customer behavior, which is a core part of Drip's pitch to online stores trying to unify their messaging strategy rather than run separate tools for email and SMS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Drip Is For
paragraphs: Drip is aimed squarely at online stores, particularly those running on Shopify or WooCommerce, that want to go beyond basic email blasts and into behavior-driven automation. Direct-to-consumer brands with recurring purchase cycles, cart abandonment problems, or a need for post-purchase upsell sequences tend to get the most value from the platform's segmentation and workflow depth.

Marketing teams that already understand customer lifecycle concepts, such as win-back campaigns, browse abandonment, and customer lifetime value tiers, will find Drip's tools map closely to those strategies. It also suits growing stores that have outgrown simpler tools like Mailchimp or Klaviyo's entry tiers and need finer control over triggers and segment logic.

Agencies and consultants managing multiple e-commerce clients are another good fit, since the platform supports multiple sub-accounts and exposes an open API for building custom integrations or reporting on top of the core product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Businesses outside of e-commerce, such as B2B software companies, service providers, or content publishers, are not the intended audience. Drip's segmentation model, reporting, and templates are built around store and order data, so the value proposition weakens quickly for anyone without a transactional storefront feeding it information.

Solo founders or very small stores just starting out with email marketing may find Drip more platform than they need. There is no permanently free plan, only a time-limited trial with a low sending cap, and the automation builder has a real learning curve, so teams wanting a simple newsletter tool with a gentle setup process will likely be better served elsewhere.

Budget-conscious merchants with rapidly growing lists should also weigh the cost curve carefully. Because pricing scales with contact count rather than usage or feature tier, a store's bill can rise significantly as its list grows, even if the merchant doesn't need the more advanced automation features unlocked at every level.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At the center of Drip is its visual workflow builder, which lets marketers construct multi-step automations triggered by specific behaviors: a product view, a cart addition, a completed purchase, or a change in customer lifetime value. Workflows can branch and combine conditions, and the platform ships with dozens of pre-built automation templates for common e-commerce scenarios like abandoned cart recovery and post-purchase follow-up.

Segmentation in Drip is built on real-time store data rather than static list uploads, letting teams build audiences from purchase history, browsing activity, order frequency, and engagement signals. SMS sits alongside email as a first-class channel with its own compliance settings and billing, so a single automation can message a customer by email, then follow up by text based on how they respond.

Beyond automation, Drip includes on-site popups and forms for list growth, revenue attribution reporting tied back to specific campaigns and workflows, and an open API for custom integrations. Every published pricing tier includes the full feature set, so smaller stores are not locked out of advanced automation or segmentation capabilities that competitors sometimes reserve for higher-priced plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Drip Experience
paragraphs: Setting up Drip typically starts with connecting a Shopify or WooCommerce store, which syncs historical orders and the product catalog before enabling behavior-based triggers. New users generally lean on the pre-built workflow templates first, adjusting timing, copy, and conditions rather than building automations from a blank canvas, which shortens the path to a working abandoned-cart or welcome sequence.

The interface rewards familiarity with marketing automation concepts more than plug-and-play simplicity. Multi-branch workflows, nested conditional logic, and detailed segment builders give experienced marketers a lot of room to fine-tune campaigns, but the same depth can feel dense to someone used to simpler email tools.

Ongoing use tends to center on refining segments and workflows as more customer data accumulates, combined with regular review of the revenue attribution reports to see which automations are actually driving sales. Support is available by email on all paid plans, with live chat reserved for higher-tier subscriptions, so response times and hands-on help can vary depending on which plan a store is on.

features.title: Visual workflow builder
features.description: Build multi-step, branching automations triggered by browsing, cart, and purchase behavior without writing code.

features.title: Behavioral segmentation
features.description: Segment customers in real time using purchase history, browsing activity, order frequency, and customer lifetime value.

features.title: Native SMS and email in one workflow
features.description: Send email and text messages from the same automation canvas, sequencing across channels based on customer response.

features.title: Shopify and WooCommerce integrations
features.description: Sync store data including orders, customers, and product catalogs in near real time via native connectors.

features.title: Pre-built automation templates
features.description: Start from ready-made workflows for abandoned cart recovery, welcome series, win-back campaigns, and post-purchase follow-up.

features.title: On-site popups and forms
features.description: Grow email and SMS lists directly on a storefront with customizable opt-in forms and popups.

features.title: Revenue attribution reporting
features.description: Track which campaigns and workflows are driving actual sales, not just opens and clicks.

features.title: Open API and sub-accounts
features.description: Connect custom tools via API access and manage multiple client or brand accounts from one login.

pros: Deep, e-commerce-specific automation with granular behavioral triggers
SMS and email run natively in the same workflow rather than as separate tools
Real-time Shopify and WooCommerce data sync powers accurate segmentation
Full feature set available on every paid tier, with no gating of advanced tools
Large library of pre-built workflow templates speeds up initial setup
cons: No permanently free plan, only a limited-time trial with a low sending cap
Noticeable learning curve for teams new to marketing automation concepts
Pricing scales with contact count, so costs can rise quickly as a list grows
Live chat support is reserved for higher-priced plans
Limited value for businesses outside of e-commerce, since segmentation is built around store data

faqs.question: Does Drip have a free plan?
faqs.answer: No, Drip does not offer a permanent free plan. It provides a time-limited free trial with restrictions on email sends and daily campaigns; ongoing use requires a paid subscription.

faqs.question: What e-commerce platforms does Drip integrate with?
faqs.answer: Drip integrates natively with Shopify and WooCommerce, syncing customer, order, and product data in near real time to power its segmentation and automation features.

faqs.question: Does Drip support SMS marketing?
faqs.answer: Yes. SMS is built into Drip as a native channel with its own compliance settings, allowing automations to combine email and text messages in a single workflow.

faqs.question: How is Drip priced?
faqs.answer: Drip uses contact-based pricing, with the monthly cost determined by how many contacts are in an account. All published tiers include unlimited email sends and the full feature set.

faqs.question: Is Drip good for beginners?
faqs.answer: Drip can be challenging for users new to marketing automation, since its segmentation and workflow builder are more advanced than simple newsletter tools. Pre-built templates help shorten the learning curve.

use_cases.title: Abandoned cart recovery for Shopify stores
use_cases.description: Automatically trigger a sequence of emails and texts when a shopper adds items to a cart but doesn't complete checkout, recovering revenue that would otherwise be lost.

use_cases.title: Lifecycle segmentation for WooCommerce brands
use_cases.description: Group customers by purchase frequency, order value, and browsing behavior to send more relevant campaigns instead of one-size-fits-all newsletters.

use_cases.title: Post-purchase upsell and cross-sell sequences
use_cases.description: Follow up after a purchase with targeted product recommendations and replenishment reminders based on what a customer actually bought.

use_cases.title: Coordinated email and SMS campaigns
use_cases.description: Run a single automation that messages customers by email first, then follows up by text if there's no response, keeping messaging consistent across channels.

pricing_plans.billing_cycle: Monthly or annual (annual billing offers a discount)
Monthly or annual (annual billing offers a discount)
features: All core automation and segmentation features
